celebration: Leaf #10 — final blends I (-nd, -nt, -mp, -st). The HARDEST leaf for the EAL room, and a big one (+62 words). Two sounds stuck together at the END of a word — the class learns to land the ending, not drop it.

songs.title: Hand-Hand-Hand!
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
Hand! Hand! Hand-hand-hand!
(nd… nd… nd…)

[Hook]
Two sounds at the END — stuck together!
/n/ and /d/ — nd! nd!
Hand-hand-hand! Hand-hand-hand!
HAND! (clap your HAND!) HAND!

[Verse 2]
Went-went-went! /n/ and /t/ — nt!
Feel the end — nt! nt!
Went-went-went! WENT!
Off it WENT! (whoosh!) WENT!

[Verse 3]
Jump-jump-jump! /m/ and /p/ — mp!
Feel the end — mp! mp!
Jump-jump-jump! JUMP!
UP you JUMP! (boing!) JUMP!

[Verse 4]
Nest-nest-nest! /s/ and /t/ — st!
Feel the end — st! st!
Nest-nest-nest! NEST!
In the NEST! (tweet!) NEST!

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! No end blend!
(nd… nd… nd…)

[Outro]
Hand! Went! Jump! Nest!
Two sounds at the end! (blend! blend!)
Say the WHOLE thing — no 'uh'!
Hand-hand-hand!
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: 🚨 THE HARDEST CATEGORY for the EAL room — Mandarin has ZERO coda consonant clusters, so children drop the final sound ('han' for hand, 'jum' for jump). This song deliberately stutters the WHOLE word (not just an onset) to force the ending back in, and doubles the repetition. Absolutely no schwa — it's 'nd' as one crisp end, never 'nuh-duh', but the ending MUST land (han-D). The '(nd… nd… nd…)' drills the naked cluster. Bridge from the known words 'and' and 'ant' — the class already lands those endings. Never sing letter names.

songs.title: The Best Nest
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(We can read. We can read!)

[Hook]
The best nest!
The best nest!
Jump in the best nest! (jump! jump!)
The best nest!

[Verse 2]
Chick went up! (up! up!)
Chick went in the nest!
Jump and jump and jump! (boing!)
The best nest!

[Verse 3]
Snake can rest in the nest!
Sheep can jump and land!
Land in the nest! (land! land!)
The best nest!

[Outro]
The nest… so soft… (hush…)
A potato?! (POTATO!)
In the nest?! (a potato nest!)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: Frame: 'The best nest.' Lands every core word — hand shows in the cards, went, jump, nest — plus 'best', 'rest', 'land'. Punch the final cluster on every one; that ending is the whole point. Whisper the outro, potato hits loud.

spreads.artNote: A cosy round twig nest sitting in the spotlight, lined with soft fluff, empty and inviting. High on a branch stub.
Chick standing proudly at the rim of the nest, wings spread wide in a 'ta-da', presenting it. A little banner reading nothing but pride.
Chick scrambling up the branch toward the nest, determined, tiny claws gripping, tail feathers straining. The climb.
Chick settling into the nest with a blissful sigh, only her head and beak visible over the fluffy rim. Perfect fit.
Chick bouncing up and down inside the nest like a trampoline, fluff flying, pure joy. An invitation to the others.

spreads.text: Snake can rest in the nest.
spreads.artNote: Snake coiled up neatly beside Chick in the nest, amber eyes half-closed, resting contentedly. Two in the nest now.
Sheep attempting to squeeze into the already-full nest, wool bulging over every edge, comic overload, Chick and Snake squished but laughing.
The whole gang crammed impossibly into one small nest, faces beaming out in a happy heap, the nest stretched round like a hammock. Togetherness.
The cast peer over the rim to find a single potato has taken the best spot right in the middle of the nest, snug as can be. No noun printed — the class SHOUTS it.

sentences: Snake can rest in the nest.
extras: PATTERN TREE: leaf #10 — the hardest leaf. Read hand/went/jump/nest aloud, landing the ending hard, and hang it.

🚨 EAL KEYSTONE (Weeks 36–37): Mandarin has NO consonant clusters at the end of a syllable, so children drop the last sound — 'han' for hand, 'jum' for jump, 'nes' for nest. Do the WHOLE-WORD echo drill twice as long as usual; make the final cluster physical (clap the -nd, stamp the -mp, hiss the -st).
BRIDGE FROM THE KNOWN: 'and' and 'ant' already end in these clusters and the class says them easily — build from there ('and → hand → band → land').
NO-SCHWA but FULL-ENDING: 'hand' is not 'han-duh' and not 'han' — the d must arrive, crisply, glued to the n. Moveable alphabet: build the base, then snap the ending pair on last (ha + nd).

teacherFocus: 🚨 THE HARDEST WEEK FOR YOUR EAL ROOM. Final blends (-nd, -nt, -mp, -st) put two consonants at the END of a word, and Mandarin allows no such clusters — so the instinct is to drop the last sound: 'han' for hand, 'jum' for jump, 'nes' for nest. Fight it structurally: double the whole-word echo drills, and make every ending PHYSICAL — clap the -nd, stamp the -mp, hiss the -st. Bridge from words the class already lands easily: 'and' and 'ant' end in these clusters, so grow 'and → hand → band → land'. No schwa, but the ending must fully arrive — 'hand' is neither 'han-duh' nor 'han'. Build words on the moveable alphabet by snapping the ending pair on LAST. Go slow, repeat twice as much, and hang leaf #10 as a hard-won victory.
